draft.introduction: Metamorphoses Book 3 contains the myths of Cadmus founding Thebes, Actaeon’s transformation into a stag, and Narcissus’s fatal self-love. The Narcissus myth has become one of Western culture’s primary symbols of self-absorption.

translation.text: Narcissus, weary from the hunt, lay beside a clear spring. While he sought to quench his thirst, another thirst grew. Captivated by the image of the beauty he saw, he fell in love with a bodiless hope, and mistook what was merely a reflection for a body. He gazed in wonder at himself. ‘What I desire, I have; my very plenty makes me poor.’
translation.original_text: Adstupet ipse sibi vultuque inmotus eodem haeret ut e Pario formatum marmore signum

translator_notes.note: Narcissus’s cry inopem me copia fecit (“my plenty makes me poor”) encapsulates the paradox of self-referential desire. Plotinus later used the Narcissus myth to warn against the soul’s enchantment with material images rather than seeking the true beauty above.
